title: Writing - Part 1: Water Scarcity
description: Your class has listened to a discussion on the global water scarcity crisis and the measures being proposed to address it.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the aspects in your notes. You should explain which aspect you consider most critical for governments and international bodies to address, giving reasons for your opinion.

aspects: Climate change and changing rainfall patterns
Access to clean water in developing nations
opinions: Agriculture accounts for around 70% of global freshwater withdrawal, and much of it is wasted through inefficient irrigation methods.
Rising temperatures are disrupting precipitation cycles, causing both droughts and flooding with greater frequency.
Hundreds of millions of people in low-income countries still lack access to safe drinking water, with severe health consequences.

structure.introduction: Introduce the global significance of water scarcity and state the two aspects your essay will examine.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first aspect in detail, conveying its scale and implications.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Explore the second aspect and argue which of the two is most critical for immediate action.
structure.conclusion: Reinforce your position and suggest the kind of response needed.

content.modelAnswer:
Water scarcity is one of the defining challenges of the twenty-first century. As global demand rises and freshwater reserves come under increasing pressure, two aspects of the crisis demand urgent attention: the extraordinary inefficiency of agricultural water use and the devastating lack of access to safe water in developing nations.

Agriculture consumes approximately seventy percent of the world's available freshwater, yet a significant proportion of this is lost through inefficient flood irrigation techniques, leaky infrastructure, and the cultivation of water-intensive crops in arid regions. Reforming agricultural practice through precision irrigation, crop diversification, and investment in water storage infrastructure would yield substantial reductions in consumption without threatening food security. The technology to achieve this largely exists; what is required is the political will to incentivise adoption at scale.

Yet while agricultural reform is essential for the long-term sustainability of water resources, the more immediate moral imperative lies in addressing the lack of safe water access in developing countries. Approximately two billion people lack reliable access to clean drinking water, a situation that drives preventable deaths from waterborne disease, restricts educational attendance — particularly for girls tasked with water collection — and perpetuates cycles of poverty. Every day without clean water represents a concrete, measurable human cost.

For these reasons, I believe that equitable access to clean water must be treated as the most critical priority. Improving agricultural efficiency is vital for the future; ensuring that people have safe water to drink is a matter of justice that admits no delay.
